speaker: Ivy Reid Lewis and Charles Rogers Reid
institution: African American Museum and Library at Oakland
rights: Copyright status unknown. Contact AAMLO for permissions.
text: In your view with Ivy Lewis, the daughter of Charles Reed, talking about Blacks enrichment January 25th, 1980, in 1935. My father's family is in California history because my great-great-grandfather William Henry Galt came to California in 1861 and he was, he came over this Miss a Panama and I had always wanted to know why they came that way and my cousin wrote a book. It's called the Manacrygienicite and the introduction of that book he talks about that they were children of a slave owner and he lived in Virginia and when the Civil War broke out he didn't want, he wanted his children to be saved so he sent all of his Black children to California and he sent them over the Ismas of Panama so that they wouldn't be captured and put back into slavery and that's how they came to California and William Henry Galt was a captain in the state militia and he had all Black regiment called his Zuevis, the California Zuevis. My father was born in Angel's camp in 1898 and his mother had been born, she was born in San Francisco on a ministry which is now a alley I think but used to be a street at one time. Anyway, their family moved Angel's camp where they lived for about five years I believe and then they came back to Berkeley and my mother and father I think were married in Oakland in 1927. My mother is a British, well she was a British subject, she's a West Indian from Barbados and we moved our family which consisted of myself and my brother and mother and father and we moved to Richmond in 1935 where my sister was born and we went to Perry's school my brother and I. My father's Charles and Burrell Reed and my brother's name was Charles Reed and my sister Florence Reed we went to Perry's elementary school when basically there were only about 10 Black children I think in Perry's school and when we lived in Northridge when there were about 12 Black families it was mostly Italian and Portuguese community and about 1943 I guess I don't remember exactly. They built the Triangle Court and one day they called all the Black children into the auditorium and the principal was Mrs. Edith's, Miss Edith Moody and she told us that a lot of children would be coming to school who had never been to school with Black children that they might call us names and we were not to fight them we were to report them and that's when they built the Triangle Court and all those housing projects were built as segregated units because Triangle Court only had white people who lived in them. Okay what are the physical boundaries? The physical boundary Triangle Court is I think it's 9th Street you know it's 8th Street to 10th Street and on Lucas Avenue and that's an Iron Triangle it's a tip of the Iron Triangle it's still standing in public housing after all these years it was built as temporary war housing but still standing in city just got funds to tear it down so it's still standing after all these years but then we had a lot of racial problems in school and I don't know all the things you want to talk about just trying to generally talk about. Some of the problems after the shipyards started we had more people coming in and I remember in North Richmond we had a kind of first when we first moved to North Richmond we lived on Philbert Street and we rented I remember that we paid $10 a month rent and we rented from Joe Nappy who was our next door neighbor and then we bought a house from I can't oh let's see what are those people's names. It was an Italian family but what happened and the realtors came out because Mr. Costa who was our one of our neighbors came over here and his wife and they told us that the real estate people had approached them about selling their house because a lot of black people were going to be moving into the area and that they should all sell their houses and leave and I guess they forgot we were black we had been their neighbor all year and they said they told my mother and father they better get out because these people were kind of rough people but what we did we bought one of the houses from one of our neighbors and we stayed in North Richmond and the house was kind of a real great big rambling house and was on two and a half acres of land so we had a lot of chicken houses and two garages and during the war there was a big demand for housing so every space we had people came to ask us could they rent them. At one time we had because our house was so large we had about four families living in our house but my mother refused to rent people their chicken coops or the garages she didn't think people should live in those things and the neighborhood became all black and but the school was still integrated I guess it was until about the 50s when more black people started to move into their own triangle. What can I remember about all those years? Go ahead. The picture I have gotten is that it was all like you said before it was all sweetness and life I tell behind the shift yards and I think that it was all sweetness and life. I think that there was a lot of discrimination there weren't very many black people and very many Spanish speaking people. Spanish speaking people generally didn't complain and I remember at one time a black man was beaten to death. Well that was during the war I guess. He was a garbage man and two light men beating to death on Carlson Boulevard that kicked us head in and it was not all sweetness. The sheriff used to come out to noise rich when I remember and intimidate people and there was a lot of noise rich when it became before the war it was sort of a rural community with just a few very few people. We had when we first moved there was R.F.D. and all the children like the Barones and the people who live way out across the creek used to have to walk into Perry school but they had a place downtown they called Peter Pan it was a fountain that you could go in by ice cream and they did not allow black people in. They did not allow black people in the winners building in a lot of places in Richmond and I was very small so I don't I remember hearing my mother and father talk about it but I don't remember it too much myself. But I know that there was a lot of discrimination and as I later worked for Mrs. Perrelli and she lived in Richmond before she moved to Lafayette she was telling me that they bought a house they moved from San Francisco and I guess the late 20s or early 30s to Richmond and that the Ku Klux Klan built burned across on their lawn because they were Catholics and it wasn't all peaches and cream at all people were very prejudiced in Richmond and during the war years it was very evident because they did have discrimination in the public housing for instance. I had just some village was all white as I said I'm trying to go court and they had restricted covenants where the real estate people kept all of the black people in a certain section which was in the north of Richmond that's the only place they could get a house. They did not rent to you or sell to you in other parts of the city. There were about three or four black families who lived I think around fifth street and I'm not sure the street over on the south side and that was the pet graves and I can't remember the elephants and some of those people some of them are still here in the city of Richmond but those were like three families who lived on the south side all the rest of the black people were in north Richmond. The schools in themselves in terms of going to school you were not segregated but I can remember a lot of things that happened that actually it was a form of discrimination like the teacher would take all the children but she never took me home with her and those kind of things. Black children were generally discouraged in taking any kind of business or college courses in school they were always put into shop or cooking classes and this sort of thing. Do you think that? Well, you mentioned some instances. I guess although it came a greater problem because it was more people feeling it after the war still from what you're saying that people were quite aware there was a problem before they just weren't either able to or just for some reason didn't fight it. Well, I know that there was a lot of problems. My father died last year so if you were alive you could tell you more. I know that I remember hearing him say that he used to cash his check and there was a bar right at the corner of 16th and McDonald. He used to cash his check there but after the war started he went in one day to cash. Well maybe it was at Gabe Argus's place. Gabe Argus had a place downtown of restaurant. He used to be a city councilman and they asked him not to come in because they didn't want black people. They didn't want him to come in anymore because other black people might come in and try to cash their checks and use the bar and they didn't want them in. There's a Mrs. Thowler that I sometimes get information from. She moved to Richmond in 1943 and she has a picture of a place that used to be on the corner of Marina Way and cutting boulevard. I guess it was a restaurant and it says we do not solicit colored patronage and she was telling me the other day how the NAACP and those years was fighting discrimination. Of course I was a child so I didn't know all these things were going on. However I did from time to time feel some form of discrimination. It wasn't aware of it until I got older. However in North Richmond did change and we had a lot of problems even with the people who came in because they had different habits like being from a rural southern community than the people in Richmond. Richmond's really changed drastically after the war. They tried to maintain this whole form of discrimination but I think that one of the things was that most of these people coming here came from very depressed areas so that when they came to California they thought they were coming to some place that was better. They were not coming to a place that had the same kind of discrimination and when they found it here I think they fought it more because they felt that it should have been different. I remember when I was in junior high school we had a lot of fights and there was a lot of trouble between the black and white and one of I was in a cooking class with a girl from Oklahoma who was white and we didn't get along and so she told me that she wasn't used to going to school with black people anyway and if I wanted to change with her and take some black girls to work with me in my class she would prefer to go into another group to cook. That's about the only problem I had. A lot of people, well my mother I remember we were shopping a lot of white people would call us names and my mother got into argument once when I was about, I imagine I was about eight years old because I remember I was really frightened. We got on the bus to go to Oakland and we originally lived in Oakland and this white lady walked on the bus and she walked up to this black lady and she said never get out of that seat and let a white woman sit down and the lady started to cry and my mother told the lady not to move she said you don't have to get up and let her sit down you stand the seat and this lady proceeded to argue with my mother and threaten her and she threatened that when we got off the bus she was going to do something to my mother but anyway the lady did not get out of her seat and let this woman sit down and the woman told my mother she says an Alabama where I come from we hang niggers like you and so I remember I had my lunch pale and I thought when I got off the bus if she did something to my mother I was going to fill with my lunch pale but that's the only thing that I could really relate to in terms of really happening and you know that I saw. There were a lot of problems like that but going back to the junior high school we had so many problems and here again I was a teenager I guess I was 12 I don't remember any such meeting but I remember hearing people talk about a meeting where the white people wanted to have segregated schools and Earl Warren was the governor of California and he said that his children that he had went to school with black people and that his children were presently going to school with them and that they were going to go to school he wasn't going to have separate schools in California. What you did you I graduated in 1958 and I came to the Virginia School. What year did you graduate? I should have graduated from Richmond High School in 1948 but I did and I went to Los Angeles and I graduated from my school there but I think it might have been 47. Excuse me yeah 47 right. No 48 probably I 48 or 49 I've got to think 48 or 49. Anyway we continue to have problems and there were a lot of racial problems a lot of fights I remember that you know between the students in school and however I don't know we my father the problem is I saw these things but my father was always involved in recreation so that he was always you know I was used to playing with white children and I didn't have a lot of the problems that the other people had so you know I was just sort of like an innocent bystander looking at all these things my father was a recreation director in North Richmond for a number of years and the center is named after him there and one of the things you tried to do was my I'll tell you my father you know even though you told me about things and I guess he was upset that happened to him he never really thought like a lot of black people he always felt that if you had what it takes to go any place that you could go and that's kind of attitude he had and he would always tell the kids in North Richmond to get an education and and try to better themselves so he really thought that you know he really believed in democracy in America if you could if you had what it takes you could get ahead and that's a kind of attitude he had and he used to work as a umpire for some of the leagues and some of them city leagues and they were mostly all white people and I don't think he had any problems they most people liked him and he I never heard him really talk about except one time they asked him not to come in the bar again I never heard him very optimistic outcome I'm elected to keep you know keeps as active as he wasn't and I didn't realize when I talked to you so he if that's a kind of attitude he tried to have a as I said now that I'm older I can look back at those things but when I was younger I didn't really see them and I used to have a lot of arguments and I I did until I was in my 20s with black people who were from the South and said that you people you're black people in California or back was because in the South we have black professional people we've always had doctors and lawyers and and we've had black teachers and that sort of thing so that you really are not progressive you know you don't have to separate yeah but they don't they because they had to have separate but equal and we went you know we went through the same school system but as I came older than I began to think also that our opportunities were still different and there's where they had to develop the black professional in California and I know if you're a California you may know that when you get out of high school generally I got a job in the canry and I made very good money I know my friend used to do peace peace works sometimes make $80 a week in a canry and I worked in the fish canry and my brother worked at the nurseries and when he was 12 years old he made 12 or 14 he made something like $50 a week and then he worked for the motor company which paid very well and I don't think in the South that those kind of jobs were open to blacks in fact a friend of mine who graduated from Harvard it was a master's so he found it really hard to quit his job to go back to school because he was making very good money and but you know then he decided you know for the future he didn't always want to do work as a on an assembly line so that he needed to have extra skills and and all these things you know I didn't think about during the time but afterwards you know realize maybe that was a difference and of course the whole thing about role models I never saw a black doctor until I guess when in the war years when two black doctors came to Richmond and so I don't know I don't think that we were deprived of anything I think that there was discrimination it was subtle I don't think it was overt like it it was an assault however I think it depend on the individuals attitude and how they could assimilate our to to get along in society because my father got a pretty good job and he used to work with Olly Freeman they had a shoe shine powder I think at first it was next to the winners building and then they later years they moved up next to Jim Traversos place and he used to be on right near Travelenys when traveling was down on 15th Street and when my father would get he worked at the Pullman Yards when he'd get off there he used to go shine shoes and then on Saturdays he shines shoes and on Sunday he used to umpire all the ball games so he knew most a lot of people some people still work in the city hall my father used to umpire ball games when they play so I think my experience was a little bit different however I do remember seeing some things that were more so after the war than before the war but I imagine when we first moved to Richmond the people were very poor I think that's one of the things I my degree is in sociology and I try to relate to that um when we first moved to Richmond I guess it was after the depression we moved in 1935 and everybody was poor white people in black people so I thought that people respected one another more when they were in the same condition I remember we used to all go over to people until this lady's house and there'd be white people in black people and they'd have French bread and salami and they have Italian wine and that you know everybody sort of umpits didn't yeah but the black people still had their own churches and the white people still went to there well most of the Italian people were Catholic I guess and we had that was most the var social activity in north Richmond was going to church and people would go fishing and I remember when I was a child we used to play in the Indian Mounds of course I didn't know that they were Indian Mounds and dig up all the shells and the hair and the bones and all the other artifacts I guess we just threw them away and we used to go down um what street is that um not Willard but it's the first street as you come in north Richmond and we would go over where uh standard oil has all the settling ponds at that time the bay used to come in to north Richmond there was an inlet and we used to go swimming there we called it the slew but I think right now there's a wrecking yard sitting there and then behind that our decedling ponds and that's where the Indian Mounds were also we used to go swimming there and then there were the turners they had there only about two houses on um what is now market avenue and we used to go out there mr. Mrs. Turner had about 10 kids and they used to have uh barbecues and we would all go over there and have like a picnic and uh all to behind north which when we lived on Philbert Ave street those were the only paved roads in the county they were all dirt roads and people couldn't drive their cars out there when it rained because you get stuck and I can't remember the kids name because he used to go to school with me he lived out in a little house and it was surrounded by lettuce it was a big lettuce field and all of the fields in north Richmond it was really pretty because they were all covered with the in the spring there used to have uh the yellow mustard and poppies and all of I the railroad tracks you know that the berms the Santa Fe berms used to be covered with poppies in the springtime a lot of wildflowers in north Richmond alloofins and the baby blue eyes and we used to go out where the par ranch is I guess it's now the par ranch and I don't know if it was there then I don't remember when I was a child but we scroll out there and we call it the bullfight they had pea tortures and apple lorges and we go out and get the fruit and come home in blackberries they still have a lot of blackberries out there that's near uh sandpablo creek along the Santa Fe tracks and um I remember also when I was a child because we didn't have a bus any buses to North Richmond we used to walk downtown Richmond to Sixth Street and get the um what was it it's not AC transit it was the other key system and it was free on Saturday mornings people could ride free to Oakland and go shopping and that's where we used to go shopping and um another thing oh I remember a lot of times that in the wintertime wall cat creek used to run over its banks and flood all the way down to downtown Richmond and once when we were at Perry's school the streets were flooded so Mr. Bossum who was the um janitor had to take us across all the children across the street but wall cat creek used to just flood all over north Richmond would just be all into interdated and the lots at that time in the county were like $15 you could buy a lot my father wouldn't buy any of them because he said you never build a house out there because it was always with right and um it was really just a rural community and all the people were um they're and I mean okay I remember in the early I guess it was I don't remember so I was 39 or 40 when the oakies came and I always thought that they were really tragic people now that I'm an adult because my friend they rented some houses down from uh Mr. Sydney who used to own quite a few houses and his daughter now lived if he could talk to her she lives in Reading no but her father owned a gas station in north Richmond and a lot of rental houses on Sanford Avenue and um he rented the houses to the oakies and they all died you know like really young children were dying I guess because they had star or they were they I had a hard time I don't know which but I remember that they came and um people didn't treat them nicely or they didn't want them around and when we moved off of Filbert Street we moved on Dubos and we were running from Mrs. Saunders and behind our house was sort of a ditch alongside the southern Pacific rail lines and I can remember that that's where the hobos used to come because all those railroads run along there and the hobos would get out and they had a hobo camp and you could see them sitting out there cooking their food and tin cans and they come around and ask for a nickel or ask could they do any work and um then when I I sort of gotten if you move it's only a few blocks from where I used to live but then when I moved over there I moved by a lot of Mexican people so I started speaking Mexican and going to the Mexican dances Helen Macias who lives who works downstairs they lived across the street from us their name was Del Gideo and her brother Julio and her sister Delita were my best friends and they're both dead now but we went to school and funny I lived in Richmond and they were across the street they lived in San Pablo the county at that time because they went to San Pablo school and I went to Perry school and later they incorporated all that area so that they had to go to Richmond school I remember there I lived close into the railroad tracks and I remember when the when the southern Pacific first had its stream liners I guess 1938 we used to go out to see the stream liners come by they were the yellow and they still have them think at that time they were called the city of San Francisco or something and what else I remember when I was seven years old I belonged to the Bluebirds and our group leader was named Mrs. Brown and Mrs. Lee and we used to meet at the Episcopal Church was on the corner of this Harborway Tens Street, a Harborway and Barrett Avenue used to be Episcopal Church and we would meet there and when the world's fair opened Mrs. Brown had a Ford with the Rumble seat so she took us to the world's fair and we sat in the Rumble seat and we went over the bridge and we at the world's where they had television so that you could see the people coming over the bridge and their cars on this little I guess but now we call television I don't know what they call remember what they call it then a little bit of screen a little bit of screen and I don't remember I remember a lot of things about it you know what I saw but I was young so I don't remember any documented kinds of things I remember we went to this building that was represented San Francisco and it had this huge whale I don't know if it was made out of a ceramic or it was black and it had water spouting out of its mouth and it was a nice building and we went to another building I think which was Southern California and they had like pyramids of oranges and grape fruits and they had all kind of things we all wrote on the China Clipper what used to I think it was five dollars or something I don't remember to write on the China Clipper be in it landed and right by the world's fair on Treasure Island and my grandmother I remember also I used to go to my grandmother's house and we used to ride our bicycles and I don't know how we went but I remember when the base shore of freeway opened we rode our bicycles down the base shore of freeway to Berkeley to see my grandmother but my grandmother lived on what is now it's California street and from her living room window we could see the tower of the sun on Treasure Island and I know you can't even see the bay from from California street in Berkeley now where she lived near Ward I think and what else do I remember can't think of thing in World War II that's not generally known because and I tell people this all the time and they they tell me it's not true but I remember that it happens when we in World War II and they took all the Japanese people and then put them in intern camps or whatever they call in concentration camps that there was some Italian people in our school and what were their names Catherine and Rosie and Elsie Patrelli I think and they lived in North Richmond and you know they moved them out of North Richmond too I don't know where they they didn't turn them but I think they just made a move out of the area I think because in North Richmond the what used to be a re-manufacturing company I think they did bathtubs or something but during war time they converted into war time manufacturing I guess casings for shells and this sort of thing and our bullets whatever and they had the shipyards in Richmond so they took them away too and recently I went to reunion for North Richmond was about two years ago and her father was talking about how they moved them out of North Richmond because of the war time activities and I also remember that there used to be an orphanage I guess and in the El Srito Hills and I think it's where they now have a school I don't know the streets it's a big building they have a college there yeah that used to be an orphanage for Chinese boys because they would all I remember they all come into Richmond and they would all have on blue sweaters I think in yellow hats and that's most of the time that we saw a lot of Chinese people in Richmond most of the time as I said we used to walk a lot so we would walk to the plunge and I understand at one time but not when I was a child I don't remember that they didn't allow black people in the plunge but I've heard other people say that they did not and I also remember going up into El Srito Brandy and that was just Hills San Pablo was nothing we would go out to what is now third I guess it's called Run-Roll Road in market and there was only one two buildings and we would call that sunny side there's a feet feet storm we would call that sunny side there were no other buildings around that was just setting out there and my father said that when he was a child his father used to walk from Berkeley overtale subriding and they'd go hunting for rabbits and he remember one time his father asked him he used to beat the bushes and then the rabbits would run out and his father would shoot him and he went and hit this particular bush in a wild cat a bobcat ran out and he said there were a lot of bobcats in that area my father did oral history for the University of California so you may be able to get something from them about when he used to play ball I remember we used to come down to first street park and they'd have ball games and Richmond do you remember when that was there there was a big ball park at first at least it was big to me I thought it was large and the only kind of activities well I remember every Saturday we used to delete the and my brother and I we'd come down to the show and we'd come to the Fox Theatre and there was a Fox Theatre and a State Theatre however during the war I think we must have about nine theaters but you know we now have some in hilltop shopping center but there was the cost of theater I think and the State and the Fox and the Uptown and the studio and the Rio those are just some I can recall and the grand theater there were several theaters and Richmond during the war and it really was quite interesting to see Richmond in the war time because part of my orientation in my life prior to World War II every Sunday everybody had to go to church because that's only place we had to go and nothing we're no you know really kind of quiet very slow town but after the war started everybody worked seven days a week so that Sunday sort of got forgotten and people didn't really go to church anymore they went to the theaters are you and that's when the store started staying open on Sundays you know the grocery stores everything used to be closed on Sunday and so I think that I my orientation for doing that I do a lot of shopping now on Sundays it hasn't changed and I remember that the men used to say I could I would hear them making cracks about the women you can't tell the women from the men and Richmond because they would all have on shipyard clothes you know those hard hats cover rolls and so you'd see all these people walking down the street and you wouldn't know if they were men or women and Richmond sort of became you know people we would see all the time we didn't see anymore you know it was like you lived some like far away and you'd go downtown Richmond and I remember there was this Reverend Davis and his daughters went to school with me they came from I guess it's not Redlands but down someplace in Southern California and near Indio California and he used to stand on he was a minister and he used to stand on the corner and ask for money so when we get out of school we'd go and help collect money for Reverend Davis he was going to build a church and that church is still in North Richmond at the corner of he did built the church from money he collected from the shipyard days it's on the corner of Chesley and Truman Street I think so and let's see what else do I remember the we in North Richmond we only had one little building and Mr. Hill had a boy scout group and sometimes we go down near and have activities but most of the things we did were very limited the McGrues and they still live here they live in the layout I think that Mr. McGru helped my father find a house when he first came to North Richmond a lot of the Spanish families and some Indian families used to live down on the Santa Fe land which is now St. John's and they had housing that was free to them because they worked at the Santa Fe yards are either Santa Fe provided them that property because at one time they took land from the Indians or something I never was I never found out the correct thing about that one of the bad things I remember about living in North Richmond and this became worse as more people got automobiles but is that a lot of people used to get killed in Richmond with the railroad tracks you know people would get killed crossing for like 47th Street because there were no barriers crossing and in North Richmond a lot of people I remember one of our neighbors husbands got killed at the one on Chesley Avenue so it was always dangerous to cross the railroad tracks and then I remember when I was a child I was telling my children the other day that my father worked at Pullman Company and he never made a lot of money so he had all these other jobs but every Friday I guess Friday evening my mother used to go shopping and we're downtown Richmond on the near the corner of Harborway and McDonald's there used to be a safeway store and so she used to go shopping in there and we'd buy a whole box of groceries for five dollars and then we'd come home in a cab because there were no buses running to North Richmond they didn't start a bus until World War II or after it was sort of like 44 or something like that and there used to be a little tiny bus and you can never get on because it was always crowded with people you could turn it off because I can't

segments.text: In your view with Ivy Lewis, the daughter of Charles Reed, talking about Blacks enrichment
January 25th, 1980, in 1935.
My father's family is in California history because my great-great-grandfather William Henry
Galt came to California in 1861 and he was, he came over this Miss a Panama and I had
always wanted to know why they came that way and my cousin wrote a book.
It's called the Manacrygienicite and the introduction of that book he talks about that
they were children of a slave owner and he lived in Virginia and when the Civil War broke
out he didn't want, he wanted his children to be saved so he sent all of his Black
children to California and he sent them over the Ismas of Panama so that they wouldn't
be captured and put back into slavery and that's how they came to California and William
Henry Galt was a captain in the state militia and he had all Black regiment called his
Zuevis, the California Zuevis.
My father was born in Angel's camp in 1898 and his mother had been born, she was born in
San Francisco on a ministry which is now a alley I think but used to be a street at one
time.
Anyway, their family moved Angel's camp where they lived for about five years I believe
and then they came back to Berkeley and my mother and father I think were married in
Oakland in 1927.
My mother is a British, well she was a British subject, she's a West Indian from Barbados
and we moved our family which consisted of myself and my brother and mother and father
and we moved to Richmond in 1935 where my sister was born and we went to Perry's school
my brother and I.
My father's Charles and Burrell Reed and my brother's name was Charles Reed and my sister
Florence Reed we went to Perry's elementary school when basically there were only about
10 Black children I think in Perry's school and when we lived in Northridge when there
were about 12 Black families it was mostly Italian and Portuguese community and about
1943 I guess I don't remember exactly.
They built the Triangle Court and one day they called all the Black children into the auditorium
and the principal was Mrs. Edith's, Miss Edith Moody and she told us that a lot of children
would be coming to school who had never been to school with Black children that they might
call us names and we were not to fight them we were to report them and that's when they
built the Triangle Court and all those housing projects were built as segregated units
because Triangle Court only had white people who lived in them.
Okay what are the physical boundaries?
The physical boundary Triangle Court is I think it's 9th Street you know it's 8th Street
to 10th Street and on Lucas Avenue and that's an Iron Triangle it's a tip of the Iron Triangle
it's still standing in public housing after all these years it was built as temporary
war housing but still standing in city just got funds to tear it down so it's still standing
after all these years but then we had a lot of racial problems in school and I don't
know all the things you want to talk about just trying to generally talk about.
Some of the problems after the shipyards started we had more people coming in and I remember
in North Richmond we had a kind of first when we first moved to North Richmond we lived
on Philbert Street and we rented I remember that we paid $10 a month rent and we rented
from Joe Nappy who was our next door neighbor and then we bought a house from I can't
oh let's see what are those people's names.
It was an Italian family but what happened and the realtors came out because Mr. Costa
who was our one of our neighbors came over here and his wife and they told us that the
real estate people had approached them about selling their house because a lot of black
people were going to be moving into the area and that they should all sell their houses
and leave and I guess they forgot we were black we had been their neighbor all year and
they said they told my mother and father they better get out because these people were
kind of rough people but what we did we bought one of the houses from one of our neighbors
and we stayed in North Richmond and the house was kind of a real great big rambling house
and was on two and a half acres of land so we had a lot of chicken houses and two garages
and during the war there was a big demand for housing so every space we had people came
to ask us could they rent them.
At one time we had because our house was so large we had about four families living in
our house but my mother refused to rent people their chicken coops or the garages she
didn't think people should live in those things and the neighborhood became all black and
but the school was still integrated I guess it was until about the 50s when more black
people started to move into their own triangle.
What can I remember about all those years?
Go ahead.
The picture I have gotten is that it was all like you said before it was all sweetness
and life I tell behind the shift yards and I think that it was all sweetness and life.
I think that there was a lot of discrimination there weren't very many black people and
very many Spanish speaking people.
Spanish speaking people generally didn't complain and I remember at one time a black man
was beaten to death.
Well that was during the war I guess.
He was a garbage man and two light men beating to death on Carlson Boulevard that kicked
us head in and it was not all sweetness.
The sheriff used to come out to noise rich when I remember and intimidate people and there
was a lot of noise rich when it became before the war it was sort of a rural community with
just a few very few people.
We had when we first moved there was R.F.D. and all the children like the Barones and the
people who live way out across the creek used to have to walk into Perry school but they
had a place downtown they called Peter Pan it was a fountain that you could go in by ice
cream and they did not allow black people in.
They did not allow black people in the winners building in a lot of places in Richmond and
I was very small so I don't I remember hearing my mother and father talk about it but I
don't remember it too much myself.
But I know that there was a lot of discrimination and as I later worked for Mrs. Perrelli and
she lived in Richmond before she moved to Lafayette she was telling me that they bought a house
they moved from San Francisco and I guess the late 20s or early 30s to Richmond and that
the Ku Klux Klan built burned across on their lawn because they were Catholics and it
wasn't all peaches and cream at all people were very prejudiced in Richmond and during
the war years it was very evident because they did have discrimination in the public housing
for instance.
I had just some village was all white as I said I'm trying to go court and they had restricted
covenants where the real estate people kept all of the black people in a certain section
which was in the north of Richmond that's the only place they could get a house.
They did not rent to you or sell to you in other parts of the city.
There were about three or four black families who lived I think around fifth street and
I'm not sure the street over on the south side and that was the pet graves and I can't
remember the elephants and some of those people some of them are still here in the city
of Richmond but those were like three families who lived on the south side all the rest of
the black people were in north Richmond.
The schools in themselves in terms of going to school you were not segregated but I can
remember a lot of things that happened that actually it was a form of discrimination like
the teacher would take all the children but she never took me home with her and those
kind of things.
Black children were generally discouraged in taking any kind of business or college courses
in school they were always put into shop or cooking classes and this sort of thing.
Do you think that?
Well, you mentioned some instances.
I guess although it came a greater problem because it was more people feeling it after
the war still from what you're saying that people were quite aware there was a problem
before they just weren't either able to or just for some reason didn't fight it.
Well, I know that there was a lot of problems.
My father died last year so if you were alive you could tell you more.
I know that I remember hearing him say that he used to cash his check and there was a
bar right at the corner of 16th and McDonald.
He used to cash his check there but after the war started he went in one day to cash.
Well maybe it was at Gabe Argus's place.
Gabe Argus had a place downtown of restaurant.
He used to be a city councilman and they asked him not to come in because they didn't want
black people.
They didn't want him to come in anymore because other black people might come in and try
to cash their checks and use the bar and they didn't want them in.
There's a Mrs. Thowler that I sometimes get information from.
She moved to Richmond in 1943 and she has a picture of a place that used to be on the
corner of Marina Way and cutting boulevard.
I guess it was a restaurant and it says we do not solicit colored patronage and she
was telling me the other day how the NAACP and those years was fighting discrimination.
Of course I was a child so I didn't know all these things were going on.
However I did from time to time feel some form of discrimination.
It wasn't aware of it until I got older.
However in North Richmond did change and we had a lot of problems even with the people
who came in because they had different habits like being from a rural southern community
than the people in Richmond.
Richmond's really changed drastically after the war.
They tried to maintain this whole form of discrimination but I think that one of the
things was that most of these people coming here came from very depressed areas so that
when they came to California they thought they were coming to some place that was better.
They were not coming to a place that had the same kind of discrimination and when they
found it here I think they fought it more because they felt that it should have been
different.
I remember when I was in junior high school we had a lot of fights and there was a lot
of trouble between the black and white and one of I was in a cooking class with a girl
from Oklahoma who was white and we didn't get along and so she told me that she wasn't
used to going to school with black people anyway and if I wanted to change with her and
take some black girls to work with me in my class she would prefer to go into another group
to cook.
That's about the only problem I had.
A lot of people, well my mother I remember we were shopping a lot of white people would
call us names and my mother got into argument once when I was about, I imagine I was about
eight years old because I remember I was really frightened.
We got on the bus to go to Oakland and we originally lived in Oakland and this white lady
walked on the bus and she walked up to this black lady and she said never get out of
that seat and let a white woman sit down and the lady started to cry and my mother told
the lady not to move she said you don't have to get up and let her sit down you stand
the seat and this lady proceeded to argue with my mother and threaten her and she threatened
that when we got off the bus she was going to do something to my mother but anyway the lady
did not get out of her seat and let this woman sit down and the woman told my mother she
says an Alabama where I come from we hang niggers like you and so I remember I had my lunch
pale and I thought when I got off the bus if she did something to my mother I was going
to fill with my lunch pale but that's the only thing that I could really relate to in terms
of really happening and you know that I saw.
There were a lot of problems like that but going back to the junior high school we had
so many problems and here again I was a teenager I guess I was 12 I don't remember any such
meeting but I remember hearing people talk about a meeting where the white people wanted
to have segregated schools and Earl Warren was the governor of California and he said
that his children that he had went to school with black people and that his children were
presently going to school with them and that they were going to go to school he wasn't
going to have separate schools in California.
What you did you I graduated in 1958 and I came to the Virginia School.
What year did you graduate?
I should have graduated from Richmond High School in 1948 but I did and I went to Los Angeles
and I graduated from my school there but I think it might have been 47.
Excuse me yeah 47 right.
No 48 probably I 48 or 49 I've got to think 48 or 49.
Anyway we continue to have problems and there were a lot of racial problems a lot of fights
I remember that you know between the students in school and however I don't know we my father
the problem is I saw these things but my father was always involved in recreation so that
he was always you know I was used to playing with white children and I didn't have a lot
of the problems that the other people had so you know I was just sort of like an innocent
bystander looking at all these things my father was a recreation director in North Richmond
for a number of years and the center is named after him there and one of the things you
tried to do was my I'll tell you my father you know even though you told me about things
and I guess he was upset that happened to him he never really thought like a lot of black
people he always felt that if you had what it takes to go any place that you could go and
that's kind of attitude he had and he would always tell the kids in North Richmond to get
an education and and try to better themselves so he really thought that you know he really
believed in democracy in America if you could if you had what it takes you could get ahead
and that's a kind of attitude he had and he used to work as a umpire for some of the
leagues and some of them city leagues and they were mostly all white people and I don't think
he had any problems they most people liked him and he I never heard him really talk about
except one time they asked him not to come in the bar again I never heard him
very optimistic outcome I'm elected to keep you know keeps as active as he wasn't and I didn't
realize when I talked to you so he if that's a kind of attitude he tried to have a as I said
now that I'm older I can look back at those things but when I was younger I didn't really see
them and I used to have a lot of arguments and I I did until I was in my 20s with black people
who were from the South and said that you people you're black people in California or back was
because in the South we have black professional people we've always had doctors and lawyers and
and we've had black teachers and that sort of thing so that you really are not progressive you
know you don't have to separate yeah but they don't they because they had to have separate
but equal and we went you know we went through the same school system but as I came older than
I began to think also that our opportunities were still different and there's where they had to
develop the black professional in California and I know if you're a California you may know that
when you get out of high school generally I got a job in the canry and I made very good money I
know my friend used to do peace peace works sometimes make $80 a week in a canry and I worked in
the fish canry and my brother worked at the nurseries and when he was 12 years old he made
12 or 14 he made something like $50 a week and then he worked for the motor company which paid
very well and I don't think in the South that those kind of jobs were open to blacks in fact a
friend of mine who graduated from Harvard it was a master's so he found it really hard to quit his
job to go back to school because he was making very good money and but you know then he decided you
know for the future he didn't always want to do work as a on an assembly line so that he needed to
have extra skills and and all these things you know I didn't think about during the time but
afterwards you know realize maybe that was a difference and of course the whole thing about
role models I never saw a black doctor until I guess when in the war years when two black doctors
came to Richmond and so I don't know I don't think that we were deprived of anything I think that
there was discrimination it was subtle I don't think it was overt like it it was an assault however
I think it depend on the individuals attitude and how they could assimilate our to to get along
in society because my father got a pretty good job and he used to work with Olly Freeman they had
a shoe shine powder I think at first it was next to the winners building and then they later years they
moved up next to Jim Traversos place and he used to be on right near Travelenys when traveling was
down on 15th Street and when my father would get he worked at the Pullman Yards when he'd get
off there he used to go shine shoes and then on Saturdays he shines shoes and on Sunday he used
to umpire all the ball games so he knew most a lot of people some people still work in the city hall
my father used to umpire ball games when they play so I think my experience was a little bit different
however I do remember seeing some things that were more so after the war than before the war
but I imagine when we first moved to Richmond the people were very poor I think that's one of
the things I my degree is in sociology and I try to relate to that um when we first moved to
Richmond I guess it was after the depression we moved in 1935 and everybody was poor white people
in black people so I thought that people respected one another more when they were in the same condition
I remember we used to all go over to people until this lady's house and there'd be white people
in black people and they'd have French bread and salami and they have Italian wine and that
you know everybody sort of umpits didn't yeah but the black people still had their own churches
and the white people still went to there well most of the Italian people were Catholic I guess
and we had that was most the var social activity in north Richmond was going to church
and people would go fishing and I remember when I was a child we used to play in the Indian
Mounds of course I didn't know that they were Indian Mounds and dig up all the shells and the
hair and the bones and all the other artifacts I guess we just threw them away and we used to go down
um what street is that um not Willard but it's the first street as you come in north
Richmond and we would go over where uh standard oil has all the settling ponds at that time the
bay used to come in to north Richmond there was an inlet and we used to go swimming there we
called it the slew but I think right now there's a wrecking yard sitting there and then behind that
our decedling ponds and that's where the Indian Mounds were also we used to go swimming there
and then there were the turners they had there only about two houses on um what is now market avenue
and we used to go out there mr. Mrs. Turner had about 10 kids and they used to have uh
barbecues and we would all go over there and have like a picnic and uh all to
behind north which when we lived on Philbert Ave street those were the only paved roads in the county
they were all dirt roads and people couldn't drive their cars out there when it rained because you
get stuck and I can't remember the kids name because he used to go to school with me he lived out
in a little house and it was surrounded by lettuce it was a big lettuce field and all of the
fields in north Richmond it was really pretty because they were all covered with the in the spring
there used to have uh the yellow mustard and poppies and all of I the railroad tracks you know
that the berms the Santa Fe berms used to be covered with poppies in the springtime a lot of
wildflowers in north Richmond alloofins and the baby blue eyes and we used to go out where the
par ranch is I guess it's now the par ranch and I don't know if it was there then I don't remember
when I was a child but we scroll out there and we call it the bullfight they had pea tortures
and apple lorges and we go out and get the fruit and come home in blackberries they still have a
lot of blackberries out there that's near uh sandpablo creek along the Santa Fe tracks and um
I remember also when I was a child because we didn't have a bus any buses to North Richmond we
used to walk downtown Richmond to Sixth Street and get the um what was it it's not AC transit it
was the other key system and it was free on Saturday mornings people could ride free to Oakland
and go shopping and that's where we used to go shopping and um another thing oh I remember a
lot of times that in the wintertime wall cat creek used to run over its banks and flood all
the way down to downtown Richmond and once when we were at Perry's school the streets were flooded
so Mr. Bossum who was the um janitor had to take us across all the children across the street
but wall cat creek used to just flood all over north Richmond would just be all into
interdated and the lots at that time in the county were like $15 you could buy a lot my father
wouldn't buy any of them because he said you never build a house out there because it was always with
right and um it was really just a rural community and all the people were um they're and I mean
okay I remember in the early I guess it was I don't remember so I was 39 or 40 when the
oakies came and I always thought that they were really tragic people now that I'm an adult because
my friend they rented some houses down from uh Mr. Sydney who used to own quite a few houses and his
daughter now lived if he could talk to her she lives in Reading no but her father owned a gas station
in north Richmond and a lot of rental houses on Sanford Avenue and um he rented the houses to the
oakies and they all died you know like really young children were dying I guess because they had
star or they were they I had a hard time I don't know which but I remember that they came and
um people didn't treat them nicely or they didn't want them around and when we moved off of
Filbert Street we moved on Dubos and we were running from Mrs. Saunders and behind our house
was sort of a ditch alongside the southern Pacific rail lines and I can remember that that's
where the hobos used to come because all those railroads run along there and the hobos would get
out and they had a hobo camp and you could see them sitting out there cooking their food and tin cans
and they come around and ask for a nickel or ask could they do any work and um then when I I sort of
gotten if you move it's only a few blocks from where I used to live but then when I moved over there
I moved by a lot of Mexican people so I started speaking Mexican and going to the Mexican dances
Helen Macias who lives who works downstairs they lived across the street from us their name was Del
Gideo and her brother Julio and her sister Delita were my best friends and they're both dead now
but we went to school and funny I lived in Richmond and they were across the street they lived in
San Pablo the county at that time because they went to San Pablo school and I went to Perry school
and later they incorporated all that area so that they had to go to Richmond school
I remember there I lived close into the railroad tracks and I remember when the
when the southern Pacific first had its stream liners I guess 1938 we used to go out to see
the stream liners come by they were the yellow and they still have them think at that time they
were called the city of San Francisco or something and what else I remember when I was seven years old
I belonged to the Bluebirds and our group leader was named Mrs. Brown and Mrs. Lee and we used
to meet at the Episcopal Church was on the corner of this Harborway Tens Street, a Harborway
and Barrett Avenue used to be Episcopal Church and we would meet there and when the
world's fair opened Mrs. Brown had a Ford with the Rumble seat so she took us to the world's
fair and we sat in the Rumble seat and we went over the bridge and we at the world's where they
had television so that you could see the people coming over the bridge and their cars on this little
I guess but now we call television I don't know what they call remember what they call it then
a little bit of screen a little bit of screen and I don't remember I remember a lot of things about
it you know what I saw but I was young so I don't remember any documented kinds of things I remember
we went to this building that was represented San Francisco and it had this huge whale I don't
know if it was made out of a ceramic or it was black and it had water spouting out of its mouth
and it was a nice building and we went to another building I think which was Southern California
and they had like pyramids of oranges and grape fruits and they had all kind of things we all
wrote on the China Clipper what used to I think it was five dollars or something I don't remember
to write on the China Clipper be in it landed and right by the world's fair on Treasure Island
and my grandmother I remember also I used to go to my grandmother's house and we used to ride our
bicycles and I don't know how we went but I remember when the base shore of freeway opened we
rode our bicycles down the base shore of freeway to Berkeley to see my grandmother but my grandmother
lived on what is now it's California street and from her living room window we could see the tower
of the sun on Treasure Island and I know you can't even see the bay from from California street
in Berkeley now where she lived near Ward I think and what else do I remember
can't think of thing in World War II that's not generally known because and I tell people this
all the time and they they tell me it's not true but I remember that it happens when we in World
War II and they took all the Japanese people and then put them in intern camps or whatever they call
in concentration camps that there was some Italian people in our school and what were their names
Catherine and Rosie and Elsie Patrelli I think and they lived in North Richmond and you know they
moved them out of North Richmond too I don't know where they they didn't turn them but I think they
just made a move out of the area I think because in North Richmond the what used to be a re-manufacturing
company I think they did bathtubs or something but during war time they converted into war time
manufacturing I guess casings for shells and this sort of thing and our bullets whatever and they
had the shipyards in Richmond so they took them away too and recently I went to reunion
for North Richmond was about two years ago and her father was talking about how they moved
them out of North Richmond because of the war time activities and I also remember that there
used to be an orphanage I guess and in the El Srito Hills and I think it's where they now have a
school I don't know the streets it's a big building they have a college there yeah that used to be
an orphanage for Chinese boys because they would all I remember they all come into Richmond and they
would all have on blue sweaters I think in yellow hats and that's most of the time that we saw a
lot of Chinese people in Richmond most of the time as I said we used to walk a lot so we would
walk to the plunge and I understand at one time but not when I was a child I don't remember that
they didn't allow black people in the plunge but I've heard other people say that they did not
and I also remember going up into El Srito Brandy and that was just Hills
San Pablo was nothing we would go out to what is now third I guess it's called Run-Roll Road
in market and there was only one two buildings and we would call that sunny side there's a feet
feet storm we would call that sunny side there were no other buildings around that was just
setting out there and my father said that when he was a child his father used to walk from Berkeley
overtale subriding and they'd go hunting for rabbits and he remember one time his father
asked him he used to beat the bushes and then the rabbits would run out and his father would shoot
him and he went and hit this particular bush in a wild cat a bobcat ran out and he said there
were a lot of bobcats in that area my father did oral history for the University of California
so you may be able to get something from them about when he used to play ball I remember we used
to come down to first street park and they'd have ball games and Richmond do you remember when that
was there there was a big ball park at first at least it was big to me I thought it was large and
the only kind of activities well I remember every Saturday we used to delete the and my brother and I
we'd come down to the show and we'd come to the Fox Theatre and there was a Fox Theatre and a
State Theatre however during the war I think we must have about nine theaters but you know we
now have some in hilltop shopping center but there was the cost of theater I think and the
State and the Fox and the Uptown and the studio and the Rio those are just some I can recall
and the grand theater there were several theaters and Richmond during the war and it really was
quite interesting to see Richmond in the war time because part of my orientation in my life
prior to World War II every Sunday everybody had to go to church because that's only place we had
to go and nothing we're no you know really kind of quiet very slow town but after the war started
everybody worked seven days a week so that Sunday sort of got forgotten and people didn't really
go to church anymore they went to the theaters are you and that's when the store started staying
open on Sundays you know the grocery stores everything used to be closed on Sunday and so I
think that I my orientation for doing that I do a lot of shopping now on Sundays it hasn't changed
and I remember that the men used to say I could I would hear them making cracks about the women
you can't tell the women from the men and Richmond because they would all have on shipyard clothes
you know those hard hats cover rolls and so you'd see all these people walking down the street and
you wouldn't know if they were men or women and Richmond sort of became you know people we would see
all the time we didn't see anymore you know it was like you lived some like far away and you'd go
downtown Richmond and I remember there was this Reverend Davis and his daughters went to school with
me they came from I guess it's not Redlands but down someplace in Southern California and near
Indio California and he used to stand on he was a minister and he used to stand on the corner
and ask for money so when we get out of school we'd go and help collect money for Reverend Davis
he was going to build a church and that church is still in North Richmond at the corner of he did
built the church from money he collected from the shipyard days it's on the corner of Chesley and
Truman Street I think so and let's see what else do I remember the we in North Richmond we only
had one little building and Mr. Hill had a boy scout group and sometimes we go down near and have
activities but most of the things we did were very limited the McGrues and they still live here they
live in the layout I think that Mr. McGru helped my father find a house when he first came to
North Richmond a lot of the Spanish families and some Indian families used to live down on the
Santa Fe land which is now St. John's and they had housing that was free to them because they
worked at the Santa Fe yards are either Santa Fe provided them that property because at one time
they took land from the Indians or something I never was I never found out the correct thing about
that one of the bad things I remember about living in North Richmond and this became worse as more
people got automobiles but is that a lot of people used to get killed in Richmond with the railroad
tracks you know people would get killed crossing for like 47th Street because there were no
barriers crossing and in North Richmond a lot of people I remember one of our neighbors husbands
got killed at the one on Chesley Avenue so it was always dangerous to cross the railroad tracks
and then I remember when I was a child I was telling my children the other day that my father worked
at Pullman Company and he never made a lot of money so he had all these other jobs but every
Friday I guess Friday evening my mother used to go shopping and we're downtown Richmond
on the near the corner of Harborway and McDonald's there used to be a safeway store and so she used
to go shopping in there and we'd buy a whole box of groceries for five dollars and then we'd come
home in a cab because there were no buses running to North Richmond they didn't start a bus until
World War II or after it was sort of like 44 or something like that and there used to be a little tiny
bus and you can never get on because it was always crowded with people you could turn it off because I can't

note: Charles Rogers Reid was a Negro League baseball player. Side A.
